title: Wet-felted pod vessel
subtitle: An organic seed-pod bowl formed over a balloon resist
excerpt: Work merino roving around an inflated balloon to build a narrow-mouthed pod shape. Once the felt has firmed up, the balloon is removed to reveal a hollow vessel suited to holding small objects, dried flowers, or decorative stones.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional wet-felt vessel technique. Balloon-resist vessel making is a well-established method in British and Scandinavian felting traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A shape placed inside the wet-felt layers during the felting process that prevents the two sides from bonding together. Once the felt is firm, the resist is removed to leave a hollow form.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon with the staples lying roughly parallel. It is laid in overlapping layers to build up the wall thickness of a wet-felted vessel.
The vigorous working stage that follows initial felting, in which the fibres are rubbed, thrown, or rolled to shrink the felt and increase its density and strength.

content.text: A pod vessel is built around an inflated balloon that acts as a
resist
. The balloon is left slightly under-inflated so it has a rounded, slightly elongated egg shape. Merino
roving

is wrapped around the balloon in layers, wetted, and worked until it becomes a stable shell. Removing the balloon reveals a hollow pod. The opening is the small gap at the top of the balloon knot end. Plan for the finished vessel to be about 30 to 40 percent smaller than the balloon due to shrinkage.

items.name: Round balloon, inflated to roughly tennis-ball size

content.text: Method
Step 1: Wrap the balloon

Tie the balloon knot end upward; this will become the opening. Pull thin strips of merino and wrap them around the balloon, working in all directions so the whole surface is covered. Build up four to five layers total, alternating direction with each layer. Leave the area immediately around the knot uncovered; a circle about 3 cm across is the planned opening.
Step 2: Wet and soap the surface

Hold the wrapped balloon over a basin and drizzle warm water over it slowly until all layers are saturated. Add a small amount of soap by rubbing it between your palms and pressing the lather onto the surface. Turn the balloon slowly and work the lather in with flat-handed pressure.
Step 3: Begin felting

Hold the balloon between both palms and rub firmly in circular motions, rotating it constantly so all surfaces are worked equally. Work for 10 minutes at a steady pace. The surface should begin to feel less slippery and more unified; individual wisps should no longer lift easily. If the surface still separates into wisps, continue rubbing for another 5 minutes before proceeding.
Step 4: Full the vessel
Once the surface is stable, begin
fulling

by pressing and squeezing the whole balloon firmly all over for 5 minutes. Then roll the balloon back and forth on the bamboo mat under both palms for another 5 minutes. The vessel should shrink noticeably. Check the wall thickness by pressing firmly; you are aiming for about 4 to 5 mm when the felt is still damp.
Step 5: Remove the balloon

When the felt is firm and the surface no longer moves under pressure, pop the balloon with a pin through the opening at the top. Pull the deflated balloon out through the opening. Rinse the vessel thoroughly under cool water to remove all soap. Reshape the vessel by hand while it is still damp: push the sides outward, flatten the base slightly, and open the rim to the shape you want. Leave to dry upright.

items.problem: The felt has thin patches or holes
items.cause: The roving layers were uneven, leaving some areas with fewer layers than others
items.fix: Place a wet patch of merino over the thin area on the outside of the vessel (while the balloon is still inside) and felt it in by rubbing firmly. The patch will bond into the surrounding surface.

items.problem: The vessel collapses after the balloon is removed
items.cause: Fulling was stopped too early; the felt wall is too thin and flexible
items.fix: Reinsert a balloon inflated to the current vessel size and continue felting. Add more merino to thin areas and work the whole surface for another 10 minutes before removing the balloon again.

items.problem: The opening is too small to reach inside
items.cause: The roving was wrapped too close to the balloon knot during laying up
items.fix: While the vessel is still damp, cut a small circle of felt away from the rim with sharp scissors. The edge will not fray.

content.text: Finishing

Once fully dry, the pod vessel is self-supporting. The felt surface can be left plain or decorated with needle-felting accents. To add a decorative rim, needle a ring of contrasting merino around the opening once the vessel is dry.
